content.text: Why Conversational Requirements Are Different

Requirements for a conversational Claude integration cannot be written by asking stakeholders what they want. Users do not know what they want from a conversational interface until they interact with one. They underestimate the range of things they will ask, they overestimate the system's ability to infer context, and they cannot predict how they will react when the system does not understand them. Traditional requirements techniques — interviews, surveys, process mapping — produce requirements that describe the happy path but miss the 80% of conversations that deviate from it.

Eliciting requirements for conversational experiences requires techniques that surface actual conversational behaviour: what users say, how they phrase it, how they recover when they are misunderstood, and when they give up. These techniques produce an intent taxonomy, a tone specification, escalation rules, and error handling requirements — the four artefacts that a prompt engineer needs to begin building a reliable Claude integration.
The Six Dimensions of Conversational Requirements

attrs.alt: Six dimensions of conversational requirements: intent coverage, tone and persona, escalation paths, error handling, context and memory, compliance limits

content.text: Every conversational Claude integration must have requirements defined across all six dimensions before prompt development begins. Missing any dimension produces a system that works in testing but fails in production — typically because real users ask things the system was not designed to handle, or because the compliance constraints were not known to the prompt engineer.
Elicitation Technique 1: Structured Shadowing

Shadowing is the practice of observing real users performing the task that Claude will assist with, and recording every decision, question, and action in real time. For conversational requirements, structured shadowing has two phases.
Phase 1: Process Shadowing

In process shadowing, the requirements analyst observes a user completing the target task (processing a support ticket, researching a policy question, drafting a document) without any AI assistance. The analyst records:
Every information source consulted and why
Every decision point and what knowledge informed the decision
Every question the user could not answer from available information
Every place where the user spent time searching, reformatting, or waiting
Every error or correction made during the task

This produces a map of the task's information dependencies — the raw material for the intent taxonomy. Each information source consulted, each decision made, and each question asked is a candidate for a Claude integration capability.
Phase 2: Query Elicitation Shadowing

After observing the task, the analyst runs a 20-minute structured exercise: the user types every question they would ask Claude during the task, as naturally as they would type it, without worrying about whether the question is appropriate or well-formed. The analyst does not answer or evaluate — they just collect. Target: 30–50 questions per user across 3–5 representative users.
The result is a raw query dataset. Analysing this dataset reveals:
Natural language patterns:
Users consistently phrase the same concept in 3–5 different ways. The intent taxonomy must accommodate all observed phrasings, not just the 'correct' form.
Implicit context:

Users frequently ask questions that assume shared context: 'what's the rule for this?' requires understanding what 'this' refers to from the conversational history. These queries identify multi-turn context requirements.
Out-of-scope queries:

Users will ask questions the system is not designed to answer. Identifying these in advance allows the system to handle them gracefully ('I can help with X and Y but not Z — for Z, contact...') rather than returning a confusing error.
Ambiguous queries:
Some queries can be interpreted multiple ways. These identify where the system needs clarification logic: 'Did you mean X or Y?'
Elicitation Technique 2: Wizard of Oz Testing

attrs.alt: Wizard of Oz testing flow showing real user, simple UI, hidden human wizard, and observer capturing requirements

content.text: Wizard of Oz (WoZ) testing simulates a working conversational AI system using a human 'wizard' who crafts responses behind the scenes. The user believes they are interacting with the finished system; they are actually interacting with a subject matter expert who is responding in real time. The observer records everything that happens — query patterns, wizard hesitations, user frustration, escalation moments.

WoZ testing is the most effective technique for surfacing conversational requirements because it generates real conversational behaviour rather than hypothetical descriptions of it. Users type the way they actually type; they misunderstand the system in the ways they actually misunderstand; they give up in exactly the situations where real users would give up.
Running a WoZ Session
Set up the environment:
A shared document or simple chat interface where the user types queries and the wizard responds. The wizard is in a separate room or on mute. The observer is visible to neither.
Brief the user:

'This is an early version of the Claude assistant we are building. It may not always understand you correctly or give perfect answers. Just use it as naturally as you would if it were your real tool. We are testing the tool, not you.'
Give the user a realistic task:

'You need to find out our policy on expense reimbursement for client entertainment, confirm the approval threshold, and find the form to submit a claim.' The task should be realistic and require 10–15 conversational turns to complete.
The wizard responds:

The wizard (a subject matter expert) crafts responses as Claude should ideally respond. When the wizard is unsure what the ideal response is, they make a note rather than guessing — the note becomes a requirements ambiguity item.
The observer records:

Query text, response time (does the user wait or re-type?), signs of confusion (repeated reformulations, 'what do you mean?'), signs of frustration (abandoned queries, unusual phrasing), and escalation moments ('can I just talk to someone?').
Debrief:
A 10-minute conversation with the user after the session: 'What worked well? Where did you feel stuck? What did you expect to happen that did not happen?'

Run 3–5 WoZ sessions with representative users before moving to prompt specification. Five sessions across diverse user types (experienced vs. new, different departments) surface approximately 80% of the intent variation the live system will encounter.
What WoZ Testing Produces
The raw outputs of WoZ testing — query logs, wizard response notes, observer records, user debrief notes — are analysed to produce:
Intent taxonomy:
A structured classification of all observed query types, grouped by topic and sub-topic, with example phrasings for each. The taxonomy defines what the Claude integration must handle.
Edge case log:
Queries that required the wizard to hesitate, escalate, or make an assumption. Each edge case is a potential failure mode in the live system.
Escalation triggers:
The specific situations in which users asked to speak to a human or expressed frustration. These define the handoff rules.
Tone observations:
Cases where the wizard's response tone felt wrong to the user (too formal, too casual, too brief, too long). These inform the tone specification.
Elicitation Technique 3: Intent Taxonomy Workshops

An intent taxonomy workshop brings together 4–6 subject matter experts (the people who currently handle the queries the Claude integration will answer) for a 90-minute structured session. The goal is to build a complete, structured classification of all query types the system must handle — both in scope and explicitly out of scope.
Workshop Format
Query generation (20 min):
Each participant writes 15–20 queries on sticky notes — one query per note, written as a user would actually type it. No editing for 'correctness'. All queries are collected.
Clustering (20 min):

The group organises the queries into clusters of similar intent. A cluster might be 'reimbursement policy questions', 'approval threshold questions', 'form and submission questions'. The group names each cluster.
In-scope / out-of-scope sort (15 min):
For each cluster, the group decides: is this in scope for the Claude integration? Out of scope? Borderline? Borderline items are flagged for stakeholder decision.
Phrasing variation (15 min):
For each in-scope cluster, the group generates 3–5 alternative phrasings that a user might use to express the same intent. These become training examples for the intent taxonomy.
Edge cases (20 min):
What queries in each cluster are hard to answer? What would the system say if the query is ambiguous? What should happen if the system cannot find a reliable answer?

The output is a draft intent taxonomy with in-scope and out-of-scope categories, example phrasings per category, and flagged edge cases. This is the primary input to the prompt specification document.
Building the Intent Taxonomy
The intent taxonomy is the structured artefact that captures what the conversational Claude integration must do. A well-structured taxonomy has three levels:
Level
Example (HR Policy Assistant)
What It Defines
Domain
Leave and absence, Compensation, Conduct and disciplinary
The top-level subject areas the system covers
Intent category
Leave — annual leave entitlement, Leave — sick leave process, Leave — parental leave
The distinct types of questions within each domain
Example phrasings
'How many days annual leave do I get?', 'What's my holiday allowance?', 'How much leave am I entitled to per year?'
Natural language variations that map to the same intent

# Intent taxonomy structure
# Used to drive prompt design and evaluation

intent_taxonomy = {
    "domain": "Leave and Absence",
    "categories": [
        {
            "id": "leave-entitlement-annual",
            "label": "Annual leave entitlement",
            "in_scope": True,
            "example_phrasings": [
                "How many days annual leave do I get?",
                "What is my holiday entitlement?",
                "How much annual leave am I entitled to?",
                "What's my leave allowance per year?",
            ],
            "edge_cases": [
                "User asks about entitlement for part-time hours (requires hours/week parameter)",
                "User asks about carryover (policy varies by contract type)",
            ],
            "out_of_scope_notes": None,
        },
        {
            "id": "leave-sick-process",
            "label": "Sick leave reporting process",
            "in_scope": True,
            "example_phrasings": [
                "How do I report sick leave?",
                "What do I do if I'm off sick?",
                "Who do I call when I'm sick?",
                "What's the process for sickness absence?",
            ],
            "edge_cases": [
                "User asks about long-term sickness (different policy — escalate to HR Business Partner)",
            ],
            "out_of_scope_notes": None,
        },
        {
            "id": "leave-individual-balance",
            "label": "Individual leave balance check",
            "in_scope": False,
            "example_phrasings": [
                "How many days do I have left?",
                "What's my remaining holiday balance?",
            ],
            "edge_cases": None,
            "out_of_scope_notes": "Requires live HRIS integration not in scope for phase 1. Redirect to: HRIS self-service portal.",
        },
    ],
}

# Count in-scope vs out-of-scope
in_scope = [c for c in intent_taxonomy["categories"] if c["in_scope"]]
out_of_scope = [c for c in intent_taxonomy["categories"] if not c["in_scope"]]
print(f"Domain: {intent_taxonomy['domain']}")
print(f"In scope: {len(in_scope)} categories")
print(f"Out of scope: {len(out_of_scope)} categories")
for c in out_of_scope:
    print(f"  [{c['id']}] Redirect: {c['out_of_scope_notes']}")

# Domain: Leave and Absence
# In scope: 2 categories
# Out of scope: 1 categories
#   [leave-individual-balance] Redirect: Requires live HRIS integration...
Tone and Persona Specification

The tone specification defines how Claude should communicate — not what it should say, but how. A mismatch between expected and actual tone is the most common source of user dissatisfaction with conversational AI systems in enterprise settings. Users who expect a formal, concise HR assistant and receive a chatty, friendly one feel the system is inappropriate; users who expect warmth and receive clinical correctness feel dismissed.
A tone specification has five components:
Component
What to Define
Example (Internal HR Assistant)
Formality level
How formal or informal should responses be?
Professional but approachable — no jargon, no slang
Response length
Brief and direct, or comprehensive and explanatory?
Default: 2–4 sentences. Complex policy questions: up to 8 sentences with structured list if >3 items
Use of 'I'
Does the system speak in first person or third person?
First person: 'I can help you with...' not 'The system can...'
Uncertainty handling
How should the system express uncertainty?
'I'm not certain about that — here's what the policy says, but please confirm with your HR Business Partner'
Brand alignment
Any specific language or phrasing to use or avoid?
Use 'colleagues' not 'employees'. Use 'guidance' not 'rules'. Do not use 'I'm afraid' or 'Unfortunately'

The single most valuable tone specification exercise is to write 5 examples of ideal responses to representative queries, and 5 examples of responses that would be unacceptable — and why. These examples are more useful to a prompt engineer than any abstract tone description.
Specifying Escalation and Error Handling

Escalation and error handling requirements define what the system does when it cannot or should not answer. These requirements are often omitted from initial specifications and only discovered when users complain that the system 'just says it doesn't know' or 'never connects me to a real person when I need one'.
Escalation Requirements
Define three types of escalation triggers:
Topic-based escalation:

Specific query types that always require human handling, regardless of system capability. Examples: legal advice, disciplinary investigations, sensitive personal circumstances. Requirement: 'For any query that mentions disciplinary action, grievance, or performance management, the system should acknowledge the query and direct the user to their HR Business Partner by name or by role.'
Sentiment-based escalation:

User expressions of distress, frustration, or urgency that should trigger a human handoff. Examples: 'I need to speak to someone', 'this is urgent', 'I'm really upset'. Requirement: 'If a user expresses urgency or distress, offer to connect them with HR support immediately rather than continuing to answer the question.'
Confidence-based escalation:

Cases where the system is uncertain and the stakes of an incorrect answer are high. Requirement: 'For questions about individual entitlements that depend on contract type or tenure, the system should provide the general policy but recommend verification with HR if precision is needed.'
Error Handling Requirements
Four error conditions must have explicitly specified responses:
No answer found:
The knowledge base does not contain relevant information. Requirement: state this clearly and direct to an alternative resource. Never make up an answer.
Ambiguous query:

The query can be interpreted in more than one way. Requirement: ask a clarifying question rather than picking the most likely interpretation and proceeding. Example: 'Are you asking about holiday leave or sick leave — or something else?'
Out-of-scope query:

The user asks about something explicitly outside the system's scope. Requirement: acknowledge the question, explain what is and is not in scope, and redirect. Never give a partial answer on an out-of-scope topic.
Conflicting information in knowledge base:
Two sources give different answers to the same question. Requirement: acknowledge the conflict, cite both sources, and recommend verification with a human authority.
From Requirements to Prompt Specification

The conversational requirements artefacts — intent taxonomy, tone specification, escalation rules, error handling requirements — are the inputs to the prompt specification document. The prompt specification is not the system prompt itself; it is a structured description of what the system prompt must achieve, which the prompt engineer then implements and tests.
A complete prompt specification includes:
Purpose statement (1–2 sentences: what is this system for and who uses it?)
In-scope intent categories (from taxonomy, with example phrasings)
Out-of-scope categories with specified redirect responses
Tone specification (formality level, response length, persona, brand language notes)
Escalation triggers and handoff instructions (by type: topic, sentiment, confidence)
Error handling for each error condition
Required disclaimers or disclosures (e.g., 'I am an AI assistant and cannot provide legal advice')
Context and memory requirements (within-session: what to remember; cross-session: what state to maintain)
Evaluation examples: 5 ideal responses and 5 unacceptable responses with explanations

The prompt specification is reviewed by the BU lead, the compliance owner (for regulated domains), and the user research lead (who ran the WoZ sessions) before prompt development begins. Changes to the specification after prompt development starts are costly — the same discipline applies as to any requirements baseline.
Common Requirements Elicitation Mistakes
The most costly mistakes in conversational requirements elicitation are predictable and preventable:
Mistake
What Happens
Prevention
Asking users what they want instead of observing what they do
Users describe idealised, simplified versions of their queries. Real queries are messier and more varied.
Use shadowing and WoZ — observe actual behaviour rather than soliciting descriptions of it
Only eliciting happy path requirements
System handles straightforward queries well but fails on edge cases, ambiguities, and emotionally charged conversations
Run edge case sessions explicitly: ask 'what is the hardest question someone could ask this system?' and design for those
Defining scope too broadly
System cannot handle everything it claims to cover; users lose trust after receiving wrong answers on in-scope topics
Narrow scope based on what the knowledge base genuinely supports; prefer fewer reliably-answered categories over many unreliably-answered ones
Skipping tone specification
Prompt engineer guesses the tone; it does not match the brand or the user population's expectations
Write 5 ideal examples and 5 unacceptable examples before prompt development begins
Deferring compliance requirements to the end
Prompt is built, tested, and approved — then compliance review finds a fundamental problem with the output format or content
Involve the compliance owner in the requirements workshop, not just the review stage
Maintaining Requirements as the System Evolves

Conversational requirements are not a one-time artefact. As the Claude integration is used in production, the intent taxonomy must be updated to reflect new query types, the tone specification refined based on user feedback, and escalation rules revised when new edge cases appear. A requirements document that is not updated becomes a liability — it describes a system that no longer exists.

Build a quarterly requirements review into the programme governance calendar: compare current production query logs against the original intent taxonomy, identify new query categories that have appeared, determine whether they should be added to scope or explicitly excluded, and update the prompt specification accordingly. A system whose requirements evolve in step with real usage remains reliable; one whose requirements are written once and forgotten accumulates undocumented capabilities and risks.
The intent taxonomy is a living document. Every quarter, compare it against what users are actually asking. The gaps between the taxonomy and the query logs are your product backlog.
Summary

Conversational requirements cannot be elicited through standard interviews and surveys. They require techniques that expose real conversational behaviour: structured shadowing captures the information dependencies in the current task; Wizard of Oz testing surfaces how real users phrase queries, misunderstand the system, and escalate; intent taxonomy workshops produce a structured classification of in-scope and out-of-scope capabilities. The outputs — intent taxonomy, tone specification, escalation rules, and error handling requirements — feed into a prompt specification document that defines what the system prompt must achieve before development begins.

All six dimensions must be specified: intent coverage, tone and persona, escalation paths, error handling, context and memory requirements, and compliance limits. A conversational integration that goes to prompt development without all six specified will discover the gaps in production — which is the most expensive place to find them.
